_title: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 Appendix 12 — Voltage Drop in Consumers' Installations
_note: Voltage drop is calculated from the origin of the installation (intake) to the equipment in use. Total voltage drop must not exceed the limits below.

limits._basis: Voltage drop from the origin of the installation (where the supply meets the consumer's premises) to any point of utilisation, expressed as percentage of nominal supply voltage (230V single-phase, 400V three-phase).

lighting_circuits_percent.note: Applies to all lighting installations. Includes both general and emergency lighting. From origin to luminaire.

other_uses_percent.note: Sockets, fixed appliances, motors, EV charging, all non-lighting loads. From origin to outlet.

total_low_voltage_supply_percent.note: Where the installation is supplied directly from a public LV network (most domestic and small commercial), the 3% / 5% limits apply. Where supplied from a private LV source (e.g. own transformer), the limits may be doubled to 6% / 8% — but the cumulative drop from the LV side of the transformer applies.

where.mVAm: millivolts per ampere per metre — from cable rating tables

single_phase.note: mVAm value from Appendix 4 already includes both go and return conductors. Do not double the length.

three_phase.formula: Vd = (mVAm × Ib × L × √3) / 1000  or use the dedicated three-phase mVAm value
three_phase.note: Modern cable tables give separate mVAm values for single-phase and three-phase. Use the three-phase value directly when available. Otherwise multiply single-phase mVAm by √3/2 = 0.866.

mVAm_values_by_cable_size._note: Values for copper conductors. Single-phase circuits — multiply by 0.866 for three-phase. XLPE/PVC values are very similar at small sizes; diverge slightly above 35mm².

temperature_correction._basis: Tabulated mVAm values assume conductor operating temperature is at its maximum (90°C for XLPE). When cable carries less than tabulated current, conductor runs cooler and resistance is lower — actual voltage drop will be less than calculated.
temperature_correction.rule: Tabulated mVAm is conservative. For cables operating well below rating, actual voltage drop is lower than calculated.
temperature_correction.correction_when_partially_loaded: Ct = (230 + tp - (Ib²/Iz²) × (tp - 30)) / (230 + tp), where tp = max operating temperature (90°C for XLPE)
temperature_correction.note: Apply correction only if voltage drop is borderline. Most designs do not require this refinement.

long_run_strategy._basis: When voltage drop exceeds the limit, the standard remedies are:
options: 1. Upsize the cable — most common. Doubling the cross-section roughly halves the voltage drop.
2. Reduce the run length — re-route via a closer DB, add intermediate DB.
3. Move the DB closer to the load centre — most effective for large installations.
4. Use a higher supply voltage (commercial only, not always feasible).
5. Power factor correction — reduces current Ib, hence reduces voltage drop proportionally.
long_run_strategy.rule_of_thumb: For lighting circuits at 230V: 1mm of mVAm × 1m × 1A = 1mV of drop. Useful for rapid sanity checks.

lighting_circuit_office.description: Single-phase lighting, 20 × 36W LED panels, 6A circuit, 30m run, 2.5mm² T&E
lighting_circuit_office.verdict: PASS — well within limit. 2.5mm² acceptable for length.

submain_three_phase.description: Three-phase submain from MSB to L1 SDB, 80A design current, 45m run, 25mm² XLPE/SWA
submain_three_phase.downstream_budget_percent: 3% lighting limit minus 1.35% submain = 1.65% remaining for final circuits
submain_three_phase.verdict: Acceptable submain drop. Final circuits must stay within 1.65% of 230V = 3.79V drop.

long_run_failure.verdict: FAIL — exceeds 5% limit. Upsize to 6mm² (mVAm = 7.3) gives Vd = 11.39V = 4.95% — JUST passes. Recommend 10mm² for margin.
